title: if and match

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for if and match; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapters 3-5: values, control flow, and data structures; atomic topic: if and match
sources.claim: Python if/elif/else evaluates truth conditions in order, while match applies structural patterns, guards, and captures to supported data shapes rather than acting as a generic switch.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Language Reference chapters 6 and 8: expressions and compound statements including match; atomic topic: if and match
sources.claim: Python if/elif/else evaluates truth conditions in order, while match applies structural patterns, guards, and captures to supported data shapes rather than acting as a generic switch.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python if/elif/else evaluates truth conditions in order, while match applies structural patterns, guards, and captures to supported data shapes rather than acting as a generic switch.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A temperature classifier tests exact thresholds with an elif chain and has a documented branch for out-of-domain sensor values. Your goal is to implement and test boundary-complete selection using an appropriate construct. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts, indentation and main guard; pf state expressions; Operators and comparisons. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python if/elif/else evaluates truth conditions in order, while match applies structural patterns, guards, and captures to supported data shapes rather than acting as a generic switch. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A capture pattern can shadow a constant name, guards run only after pattern success, sequence/string patterns differ, wildcard placement matters, and incomplete numeric boundaries leave gaps. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Every bare name in a match case compares against an existing variable or constant value.

includes: Use conditions and structural pattern matching for complete and readable decisions.
Implement and test boundary-complete selection using an appropriate construct.
excludes: A capture pattern can shadow a constant name, guards run only after pattern success, sequence/string patterns differ, wildcard placement matters, and incomplete numeric boundaries leave gaps.

outcomes: Implement and test boundary-complete selection using an appropriate construct.

examples.title: if and match: direct language case
examples.situation: A temperature classifier tests exact thresholds with an elif chain and has a documented branch for out-of-domain sensor values.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A temperature classifier tests exact thresholds with an elif chain and has a documented branch for out-of-domain sensor values.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Treat bare names in patterns as captures unless they are qualified value patterns; choose match for structural decomposition and test every selection boundary.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Treat bare names in patterns as captures unless they are qualified value patterns; choose match for structural decomposition and test every selection boundary.

examples.title: if and match: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A match statement destructures tagged command tuples, uses literals for command names, and keeps a final wildcard for malformed shapes.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A match statement destructures tagged command tuples, uses literals for command names, and keeps a final wildcard for malformed shapes.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A capture pattern can shadow a constant name, guards run only after pattern success, sequence/string patterns differ, wildcard placement matters, and incomplete numeric boundaries leave gaps.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Every bare name in a match case compares against an existing variable or constant value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test boundary-complete selection using an appropriate construct.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Every bare name in a match case compares against an existing variable or constant value.

analogy.example: a labeled decision board whose current objects feed expressions, branches, patterns, and repeatable progress rules can introduce if and match, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: scalar semantics, comparisons, selection, and iteration
limitations: identity, truthiness, protocol dispatch, chained comparisons, and iterator behavior do not follow ordinary board-game rules
